name: Ernie Royal
abstract: Ernest Andrew Royal (June 2, 1921 in Los Angeles, California – March 16, 1983 in New York City) was a jazz trumpeter. His older brother was clarinetist and alto saxophonist Marshal Royal, with whom he appears on the classic Ray Charles big band recording The Genius of Ray Charles (1959).

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Wilder
about_quotes.evidence: So it was Karl George, Ernie Royal, Joe Newman, and me. We were the four trumpets in the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Wilder — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Wilder Part 2
about_quotes.evidence: And while we were there it was Ernie Royal, Joe Newman and I were the trumpets.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Wilder Part 2 interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 12, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Earl Watkins
about_quotes.evidence: I was working at the Band Box in Menlo Park at the time with Buddy Collette and Ernie Royal and a fella named Jimmy Shorter, a pianist, we had a quartet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Earl Watkins interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Francisco, California, August 7, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Buddy Collette
about_quotes.evidence: Ernie Royal, who’s the brother of Marshall Royal, we had a combo. I had a combo, four people.
about_quotes.doc_title: Buddy Collette interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 13, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: the trumpets in that band was Ernie Royal, Joe Newman, and Karl George.

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: Sure enough, when I get to the session, we did all the other – I did all of my other flute work, but at the very end of the session, they had this little band, like with Jerome Richardson and I think Joe Farrell and some of the other saxophone players and trum[peters], Ernie Royal and Snooky Young.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
